model_narrative: A salesman must visit a fixed set of cities exactly once each on a single closed tour that starts and ends at a home city. For every ordered pair of cities we decide whether the tour travels directly from the first to the second, and we also track the position of each city in the visiting order. The objective is to choose the tour that makes the total travel cost over all the legs actually taken as small as possible.

sets.name: I
sets.doc: the cities that must all be visited on a single closed tour; the very first member is the fixed home city where the tour starts and ends

params.name: c
params.doc: the symmetric travel cost between an ordered pair of cities; the cost from a city to itself is zero

vars.name: x
vars.doc: 1 if the tour travels directly from the first city of the pair to the second city of the pair, 0 otherwise; this is a directed arc, so the pair is ordered

vars.name: u
vars.doc: the position of a city in the visiting order, a continuous rank between 1 and the number of cities; the home city is fixed at position 1

objective.expr_var: total travel cost over all chosen arcs

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every city must be left exactly once. For each city, exactly one leg of the tour departs from it to some other city.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every city must be entered exactly once. For each city, exactly one leg of the tour arrives at it from some other city.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: The tour may never travel from a city directly back to itself. For each city, the leg that would go from that city to itself is never used.

constraints.name: I)
constraints.intent: The visiting order must rise along each leg of the tour so that no smaller disconnected loops can form among the cities away from home. For each ordered pair of distinct cities where neither city is the home city, if the tour goes directly from the first to the second then the second must come later in the visiting order than the first. When that leg is not taken the requirement relaxes and places no real restriction. The home city is left out of this requirement entirely.

query: The truck always darts straight out of the depot at stop 1 to its closest neighbor, which keeps shunting the three inland stops 3, 4 and 5 deep into the loop. Shouldn't the depot's very first leg head into one of those three inland stops instead of hugging the nearest pair? What's keeping the model from opening the route that way?

query: There's a proposal on the table to commit the depot at stop 1 to launching deeper into the territory — its outbound leg should go to either inland stop 4 or inland stop 5, rather than to the close-in stops. Plug that requirement in and tell me what the full tour ends up costing.

query: We're looking at a dispatch rule for the two depot-end stops, 1 and 2: each of them should send its outbound leg into the inland cluster — one of stops 3, 4 or 5 — instead of just hopping to the other depot-end stop. Apply that to both and let me see the resulting tour distance.
expected_pyomo:
def _early_inland(model, i): return sum(model.x[i, j] for j in ['c3','c4','c5'] if j != i) >= 1
model.early_inland = Constraint(['c1','c2'], rule=_early_inland)

query: Engineering is pitching a two-part routing change: the depot at stop 1 should launch into the inland cluster (one of stops 3, 4 or 5), and separately stop 2 should be approached from the inland side too — its inbound leg coming in from stop 4 or stop 5. Put both pieces in together and walk me through the new tour cost.
expected_pyomo:
model.c1_out_inland = Constraint(expr=model.x['c1','c3'] + model.x['c1','c4'] + model.x['c1','c5'] >= 1)
model.c2_in_inland = Constraint(expr=model.x['c4','c2'] + model.x['c5','c2'] >= 1)

query: There's a proposal to fully reorient stop 2 toward the inland stops: have the depot at stop 1 launch into the inland cluster (3, 4 or 5), have stop 2 leave toward inland stop 4 or 5, and have stop 2 also be entered from inland stop 4 or 5. Stack all three of those together and tell me what the optimizer returns for the tour distance.
expected_pyomo:
model.c1_out_inland = Constraint(expr=model.x['c1','c3'] + model.x['c1','c4'] + model.x['c1','c5'] >= 1)

query: Let's try pushing both depot-end stops outward at once: stop 1 should launch into the inland cluster (3, 4 or 5), stop 2 should likewise leave into the inland cluster (3, 4 or 5), and on top of that the depot at stop 1 should be re-entered from inland stop 4 or 5. Apply the whole package and tell me what the tour distance becomes.
expected_pyomo:
model.c1_out_inland = Constraint(expr=model.x['c1','c3'] + model.x['c1','c4'] + model.x['c1','c5'] >= 1)

query: Stop 2 is forever glued to the depot-end stops — it always hops to or from stop 1 — and the inland stops keep getting picked up much later in the loop. Shouldn't stop 2 instead leave toward one of the inland stops 4 or 5 and pull them into the route earlier? What's the model seeing that keeps stop 2 pinned to the depot end?

Shouldn't the depot be the stop that pushes further inland — its outbound leg going into the inland cluster more than stop 2's does? Wouldn't it be obvious to have the depot lead on the inland reach rather than stop 2? What's pulling the route the other way?

query: The depot at stop 1 only ever touches its two closest neighbors — it slips out to the nearest stop and gets re-entered from the nearest stop, never really engaging the inland stops at either end. Shouldn't the depot sit squarely inland instead, launching into one of stops 3, 4 or 5 and being re-entered from stop 4 or 5? What's keeping the model from anchoring the depot inland on both sides?
